content.text:
Every integral domain can be embedded in a field, just as $\Z$ sits 
inside $\Q$. This field of fractions is the smallest field containing 
the domain.

content.title: Field of Fractions
content.content:
Let $D$ be an integral domain. There exists a field $F$, unique up to 
isomorphism, such that:

1. $D$ embeds in $F$ (i.e., there is an injective ring homomorphism 
 $D \hookrightarrow F$)

2. Every element of $F$ can be written as $a/b$ for $a, b \in D$ 
 with $b \neq 0$

3. $F$ is minimal: any field containing a copy of $D$ contains a 
 copy of $F$
This field $F$ is called the **field of fractions** 
(or quotient field) of $D$, denoted $\text{Frac}(D)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Consider the set $S = D \times (D \setminus \{0\})$ with the equivalence 
relation $(a, b) \sim (c, d)$ iff $ad = bc$.

Define operations on equivalence classes:
$$
 \frac{a}{b} + \frac{c}{d} &= \frac{ad + bc}{bd} \\
 \frac{a}{b} \cdot \frac{c}{d} &= \frac{ac}{bd}
$$

The verification that these are well-defined and satisfy the field 
axioms uses the fact that $D$ is an integral domain (no zero divisors).

The embedding $\iota: D \to F$ is given by $\iota(a) = a/1$.

content.title: Universal Property
content.content:
If $\phi: D \to F$ is an injective ring homomorphism from an integral 
domain to a field, then there exists a unique ring homomorphism 
$\bar{\phi}: \text{Frac}(D) \to F$ extending $\phi$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Define $\bar{\phi}(a/b) = \phi(a)/\phi(b)$. This is well-defined since 
$\phi$ is injective and $\phi(b) \neq 0$ when $b \neq 0$.

content.title: Field of Rational Functions
content.content:
If $F$ is a field, the **field of rational functions** 
over $F$ is
\[
 F(x) = \text{Frac}(F[x]) = \left\{ \frac{f(x)}{g(x)} : f, g \in F[x], 
 g \neq 0 \right\}
\]

content.problem:
$\R(x) = \left\{ \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} : p, q \in \R[x], q \neq 0 \right\}$

For instance, $\frac{x^2 + 1}{x^3 - x} \in \R(x)$.

items:
Show that the field of fractions of $\Z[\sqrt{2}]$ is 
 $\Q(\sqrt{2})$.

Prove that $\text{Frac}(D_1 \times D_2)$ does not exist if 
 $D_1, D_2$ are nonzero integral domains (the product is not 
 a domain).

If $D \subseteq E$ are integral domains, show that 
 $\text{Frac}(D) \subseteq \text{Frac}(E)$.

marginNotes.text:
Come, all you who are thirsty, come to 
the waters.
